title: podman 4.4 with CNI backend doesn't correctly configure veth devices/connections for bridged network
body:
### Issue Description

Attempting to bridge containers to a GCE network (plain bridging, not macvlan or ipvlan). This seems to require CNI in 4.4, because host-local is being used for a limited range of IP addresses from the wider /20 network used in GCE.

Tried creating a root bridged network with --subnet, --gateway, and --ip-range with sudo podman network create. create rejects this if the full /20 subnet is specified, because the subnet is already used by the OS bridge adapter (br0 which slaves the OS eth0 adapter). create also rejects this if the --subnet is set to the /26 subset used in --ip-range, because, then, the gateway is no-longer in the --subnet range. GCE configures all hosts with /32 subnets and a .0.1 gateway, which works fine, even though the range technically has 0 addresses, and the gateway is outside the range. S

Created a CNI bridge definition (plug ins 1.2 is installed), with an ipam-defined range and subnet, dns, routes, etc. This one is configured to bridge to the OS's br0 bridged device. The range is set to host-local. When starting a container with --network=<network-name-from-CNI>:ip=<address-in-range-from-CNI>, podman creates a veth adapter, but seems to be expected a DHCP address for it (DHCP errors show up in the host's serial console).

Installed netavark and tried that instead. Results were worse. Best result was with CNI in use and a network create with a --subnet to match the --ip-range. That allowed the container to ping the host, other containers, and other servers on the GCE network. However only the host, or other containers, could ping the container; other servers on the GCE network could not ping the container's IP. With netavark and the same network create statement, the host could ping the container, and the container could ping other containers, but neither direction between the container and the GCE network worked. 

Please advise if there is a way to fully bridge containers to the host's eth0 adapter with podman 4.4.

Thanks,
Paul

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Configure the host so the main network interface is a slave to a bridge device (br0)
2. Attempt to bridge a container to the host network adapter (eth0) (where supported - i.e. CNI - using br0 as the bridge device) while using a static host-local range of IP addresses which are a subset of the subnet to which the host's eth0 is connected.


### Describe the results you received

Sometimes: Rejected network create command
Sometimes: Container not able to ping external network
Always: External network not able to ping container

### Describe the results you expected

Container interacts with internal network, using the assigned :ip=, just like the host interacts with the external network.

Container should be pingable from addresses on the host's eth0 network.

Container should be able to ping addresses on the host's eth0 network.

Container should be able to use host's eth0 network gateway to access other networks.

comments.body:
Bridge means for us to create our own bridge and attach containers to it and then NAT external connections.
Generally speaking we do not support using an existing bridge as NAT rules are always added which in your case could be the problem. If you want to use an existing device you should just use macvlan.

In general I do not really understand your setup. It would help if you could actually show what commands you are trying to run, how you CNI config looks etc... Also I have no idea about the GCE setup, the fact that using a /32 with a default route even works at all is highly questionable. Are you sure GCE routes the other ips to your VM? Did you try to set tis up with basic ip command to see if it even works in the first place?

Also you say you use CNI but podman info says netavark
>  networkBackend: netavark

Given that this is not a config that we support I am closing the issue.

I said I had tried both CNI and netavark.

Here's an article that describes how Google does their networking in GCE, including the use of /32 addresses and all traffic routing through the default gateway: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/create-use-multiple-interfaces

I am mainly blocked by two things: podman network create doesn't allow a --subnet that is the same as the host's subnet, and podman network create doesn't allow --gateway that is not in the --subnet range. The ideal solution would be if the first restriction didn't exist, then I could do:

sudo podman network create --driver=bridge --subnet 10.128.0.0/20 --gateway 10.128.0.1 --ip-range=10.128.6.64/26 --ipam-driver=host-local mynetwork

And have my own, locally managed, range of addresses for containers, but still be able to reach all of the other local and Internet resources.

Instead, I get: Error: subnet 10.128.0.0/20 is already used on the host or by another config

My current setup with a bridged network (with the subnet being the same as the range, and with no gateway) at least allows the host to interact with the containers, and the containers to interact with each other, so that may work, though it's cumbersome.

vlans are not really an acceptable alternative to bridges, since they are invisible to the rest of the network unless you add some other gateway.
